coredata.dc:title: An AEE-active probe combined with cyanoacrylate fuming for a high resolution fingermark optical detection
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yuhao
Sun, Yun
Deng, Yong
Liu, Jie
Fu, Jingxin
Ouyang, Ruizhuo
Miao, Yuqing
coredata.dc:description: Latent fingermark imaging plays an important role in forensic investigation. The choice of an appropriate method and reagent is essential for acquiring high quality fingermark images. In this work, a push-pull structure NIFA of a fluorene (π-conjugated) core substituted with 1,8-naphthalimide (electron-attracting group) and anthracene (electron-donating group) was fabricated, which provided an aggregation-enhanced emission (AEE). The photophysical and AEE properties of NIFA were investigated by experimental and theoretical studies. This dye can emit bright blue to blue-green fluorescence. The results showed that the intramolecular charge transfer from the fluorene part to the 1,8-naphthalimide part dominated the luminescence of NIFA. The combined utilization of cyanoacrylate glue fuming and a NIFA fluorescent imaging technique can image the fine structure of fingermark from the first level to the third level. After investigating the interaction mechanism between cyanoacrylate glue and NIFA, we found that the hydrophobic-hydrophobic interaction was in charge of the fingermark imaging quality enhancement. All results proved that this AEE active fluorescent probe combined with cyanoacrylate fuming could supply a new method for latent fingermark fluorescent detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: AEE
Photophysical
Fingermark detection
Cyanoacrylate glue
Naphthalimide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Protocol of fuming and incubating fingermarks

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthetic route of NIFA, (b) the illustration of NIFA emission changing from weak emission in CH3CN to aggregation enhanced bright emission enhancement in CH3CN/H2O solution, (c) the diagram of the combination method for latent fingermark fluorescent imaging.
UV–vis absorption (line) of NIFA in CH2Cl2 solution and the calculated oscillator strength (line + dot) of NIFA in gas phase.
(a) The fluorescent emission and (b) the corresponding emission digital photo of NIFA in different solvents. (c = 1.0 × 10−5 mol·L-1, λ ex = 365 nm).

(a) Fluorescent spectra and (b) plots of fluorescent areas of NIFA in CH3CN/H2O mixture with different H2O fractions (vol, 0–40%). Inset in Fig b is the digital photo of NIFA in CH3CN without and with 40% H2O. (c = 1.0 × 10−5 mol·L-1, λ ex = 410 nm).
Fluorescent latent fingermark images are captured by (a) digital camera and (b) EMCCD and the pseudo-color is added in (b). (365 nm UV lamp as the excitation source).
The SEM images of latent fingermark incubated with NIFA NPs solution without (a, b) and with (c, d) pretreated with superglue fume.

Photographs (a–d) and the corresponding fluorescent images (e–h) of fingermarks on the glass, aluminum foil, spoon and soft drink label substrates, and the enlarged second level details of fingermark fluorescent images (1–12).
The third level fingermark fluorescent image and the enlarged fine structure of ridges in (1) and sweat pores in (2).

row.entry: λ em/nm (τ em/ns, Φ em) b

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Electronic absorption band maxima and molar extinction coefficients in CH2Cl2 at room temperature.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Room temperature emission band maxima and decay lifetimes were measured in CH2Cl2 at a concentration of 1×10−5 mol·L-1.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The emission band maxima at 77 K was measured in BuCN glassy solution at a concentration of 1×10−5 mol·L-1.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The calculated lowest-energy absorption band of NIFA.

head.ce:title: An AEE-active probe combined with cyanoacrylate fuming for a high resolution fingermark optical detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Bismuth Science & College of Science, University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, Shanghai 200093, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Bismuth Science & College of Science
University of Shanghai for Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Research and Development & Department of Nuclear Medicine, Shanghai Proton and Heavy Ion Center, Fudan University Shanghai Cancer Center, Shanghai 201321, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Research and Development & Department of Nuclear Medicine
Shanghai Proton and Heavy Ion Center

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Biomedical Imaging & Shanghai Medical College, Fudan University, Shanghai 200032, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center for Biomedical Imaging & Shanghai Medical College

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Engineering Research Center for Molecular Imaging Probes, Shanghai 200032, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Engineering Research Center for Molecular Imaging Probes

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Latent fingermark imaging plays an important role in forensic investigation. The choice of an appropriate method and reagent is essential for acquiring high quality fingermark images. In this work, a push-pull structure NIFA of a fluorene (π-conjugated) core substituted with 1,8-naphthalimide (electron-attracting group) and anthracene (electron-donating group) was fabricated, which provided an aggregation-enhanced emission (AEE). The photophysical and AEE properties of NIFA were investigated by experimental and theoretical studies. This dye can emit bright blue to blue-green fluorescence. The results showed that the intramolecular charge transfer from the fluorene part to the 1,8-naphthalimide part dominated the luminescence of NIFA. The combined utilization of cyanoacrylate glue fuming and a NIFA fluorescent imaging technique can image the fine structure of fingermark from the first level to the third level. After investigating the interaction mechanism between cyanoacrylate glue and NIFA, we found that the hydrophobic-hydrophobic interaction was in charge of the fingermark imaging quality enhancement. All results proved that this AEE active fluorescent probe combined with cyanoacrylate fuming could supply a new method for latent fingermark fluorescent detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Aggregation-induced emission (AIE) is a remarkable characteristic of some unique molecules used to overcome fluorescent quenching in high concentration solutions or the solid state. Since the report of the first AIE molecule in 2001 by the group of Tang, increasing numbers of studies have focused on this exciting phenomenon. [1–5] Some AIE mechanisms have been revealed, such as restricted intramolecular rotation (RIR), intramolecular coplanarization, inhibition of intramolecular photochemical or photophysical processes and so on [6]. Among these reported mechanisms, the RIR mechanism is widely used to guide researchers to design new AIE molecules [7,8]. Based on this mechanism, some aggregation-enhanced emission (AEE) dyes have also been reported and studied [9–12]. All of these AIE and AEE molecules have been used in different fields, e.g., OLEDs [13–15], luminescent analysis [16–18], bioimaging [19–21], biotherapy [22], forensic testing [23–26], data security [27–30] and so on.

Recently, the application of AIE molecules in the field of latent fingermark has been paid more and more attention. [23–25] It is well known that identifying latent fingermark plays an important role in criminal investigation and evidence identification. Commonly, dusting with a powder method is preferable, but the quality of latent fingermark images produced in this way is limited [31,32]. Only the first level of fingermarks can be clearly observed. Even if some dyes are doped in the dust powder, the second level is not easily distinguishable. When aggregation caused quenching (ACQ) fluorescent dyes as powders are used, the fluorescence of the dye is quenched, so that the structure of the fingermark also cannot be distinguished. After replacing with an AIE powder or solution, fluorescent latent fingermark can be easily observed. Previous work revealed that the advantages of AIE dyes for latent fingermark imaging are the tunable luminescent color, high quantum yield and better imaging quality [23–26].

Although dusting with the powder method is currently being used for latent fingermark detection, some other methods are also used, e.g., staining with ninhydrin solution and fuming with cyanoacrylate glue (aka superglue, 502 glue). [33,34] Based on significant practice, fuming with superglue is one of the effective methods for latent fingermark imaging. This method can fix fingermark in situ, avoid destroying the details of latent fingermark and is easy to operate at a crime scene. [35] After fuming, the off-white fingermarks are merged on the substrate, but the contrast is lacking for identifying the details [36]. Based on the above-mentioned methods, choosing an appropriate combination method is one of the unique robust method for improving the fingermark imaging quality. The combination of superglue fuming and fluorescence has been reported to improve the latent fingermark imaging quality and capture more fingermark details [37–39]. After superglue fuming and fixing latent fingermark, many emissive materials (e.g. inorganic materials [40–45], organic dyes [38], heavy metal complexes [26,46], quantum dots [43], upconversion nanoparticles [47,48], and so on) can image the fingermark in situ. We believe this combination method is effectual, convenient and inexpensive.

Herein, we report an AEE dye (NIFA) based on the 1,8-naphthalimide structure. The photophysical and electrochemical properties of NIFA were investigated systematically. This dye can emit bright blue to green-blue emission under UV lamp excitation. Furthermore, combined with superglue fuming, NIFA can detect and image latent fingermark. The detection mechanism is investigated. More importantly, the fine high resolution structure of the first level to the third level of fingermark is visualized. Thus, the AEE active NIFA dye is expected to be a promising material for the optical detection and imaging of latent fingermark.

All chemical reagents and photophysical testing solvents were purchased from Tansoole and were used without any further purification. According to the literature reported methods, the intermediates 6-(7-bromo-9,9-dihexyl-9H-fluoren-2-yl)-2-butyl-1H-benzoisoquinoline-1,3(2 H)-dione (1) [49] and 2-(anthracen-9-yl)-4,4,5,5-tetramethyl-1,3,2-dioxaborolane (2) [50] were synthesized and directly used for the next step.

1H-NMR and 13C-NMR spectra were recorded on a Varian Oxford-500 NMR spectrometer. Tetramethylsilane (TMS) as an internal reference was used to report the proton chemical shifts in ppm down field. High resolution-mass (HR-MS) spectrum was measured on an AB Sciex X-500B QTOF-mass spectrometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Hitachi U-2900 ultra-violet-visible-near infrared spectrometer. Edinburg FS5 fluorescent spectrometer was used to measure emission spectra. Hamamatsu Quantaurus-QY system was applied for recording the absolute emission quantum yields. The emission lifetime was measured by Edinburgh ps-lifetime instrument with 355 ps laser as excited source. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) size distribution was recorded on a Brookhaven 90 Plus Zeta. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were collected by Tescan VEGA3 scanning electron microscope. High performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) was measured on Agilent 1200 with UV detector (Kromasil C18 column, L = 20 cm, H2O/CH3OH = 70:30, flow: 1 mL·min−1, λ detection = 220 nm).

The synthetic procedure was similar to the previous reported method [8] and displayed in Scheme 1 . Compound 1 (200 mg, 0.3 mmol, 1 equiv.), compound 2 (92 mg, 0.3 mmol, 1 equiv.) and Pd(PPh3)4 (21 mg, 0.018 mmol, 0.06 equiv.) were added to a round-bottom flask. Toluene (30 mL) was added. Then, K2CO3 aqueous solution (1.5 mmol, 1 mL, 5 equiv.) was added. All in flask was strongly stirred and heated at the reflux temperature under argon protection for reacting 48 h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture solvent was removed. The residue was extracted with CH2Cl2 and washed with brine. Then, the organic layer was dried with MgSO4 and removed the solvent. Crude product was purified by flash column chromatography (silica gel, 200–300 mesh). Hexane/ether = 20:1 to 15:1 (v/v) was used as the eluent, to afford 166 mg yellow solid, 72% yield. 1H-NMR (500 MHz, CDCl3) δ 8.69 (dd, J = 16.6, 7.3 Hz, 2 H), 8.53 (s, 1 H), 8.37 (d, J = 8.5 Hz, 1 H), 8.08 (d, J = 8.5 Hz, 2 H), 7.99 (dd, J = 10.6, 7.9 Hz, 2 H), 7.83 (d, J = 7.5 Hz, 1 H), 7.78 - 7.70 (m, 3 H), 7.57 - 7.52 (m, 2 H), 7.52 - 7.45 (m, 4 H), 7.39 - 7.34 (m, 2 H), 4.29 - 4.21 (m, 2 H), 2.05 (p, 4 H), 1.83 - 1.73 (m, 2 H), 1.49 (dq, 2 H), 1.20 - 1.09 (m, 13 H), 1.01 (t, 3 H), 0.93 - 0.77 (m, 11 H); 13C-NMR (126 MHz, CDCl3) δ 164.63, 164.52, 164.45, 151.74, 151.40, 147.73, 147.71, 141.70, 139.97, 138.36, 137.81, 137.57, 132.92, 131.67, 131.41, 131.10, 130.56, 130.52, 130.41, 129.22, 129.09, 128.68, 128.16, 127.01, 126.99, 126.85, 126.44, 125.61, 125.37, 124.81, 123.26, 121.90, 120.24, 120.21, 55.77, 40.57, 31.79, 30.51, 29.86, 24.27, 22.74, 20.68, 14.28, 14.13. QTOF-MS calcd. for [M+H] 762.4311, found 762.4319.

To understand the nature of the ground state of compound NIFA, quantum chemical calculation was performed. The calculation was conducted at the time dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) level of theory, in conjunction with the B3LYP functional and the 6-31G* basis set, as implemented in the Gaussian 09 program package. [51] The ground state geometry structure of NIFA in a DFT calculation is based on the gas phase optimized geometry.

For collecting the regular latent fingermark, some recognized procedures have been reported and been used. Before pressing their fingers on the different substrates, the volunteers were asked to clean their hands and then to touch their oily parts like forehead or nose region. Then the collected fingermarks were kept at least 12 h under room temperature for further use. [38,47]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Protocol of fuming and incubating fingermarks
ce:section.ce:para: The incubation procedure for the latent fingermarks on different substrates was developed following the same procedures and indicated in Scheme 1 [38]. (1) Fingermark was fumed with cyanoacrylate glue (superglue). Fingermark sample and 2 mL superglue were put in a culture dish (diameter = 15 cm). Then fully covered the culture dish lid to maintain a closed system at room temperature for 10 min. Heated the culture dish by the hair dryer could accelerate the sample fuming effect. (2) Take the fingermark sample out of the culture dish and place it on the lab table. Several drops of NIFA solution (0.2 mg mL−1, ethanol:H2O = 1:1, v/v) were dropped on the fingermark to incubate 7 min. (3) Sucked the NIFA solution away, and washed the sample with H2O and CH2Cl2 once. Then dried the sample for the following fluorescence fingermark imaging.

Portable UV lamp (365 nm, power = 8 W) was used as a light source. Nikon D90 digital camera with a Nikon AF-S lens (18–105 mm f/3.5–5.6 G ED) or Andor DU897 EMCCD on the Olympus stereo microscopes (CKX41) with band pass optical filter (442 nm long pass) was used to collect emission signals. Images collected from EMCCD were analyzed with ImageJ software to add pseudo-color.

The designed and synthetic route of NIFA is illustrated in Scheme 1. 1,8-Naphthalimide provides the electron-withdrawing ability. The fluorenyl group is commonly chosen as a π-conjugated unit. After introducing an electro-donating group, the different parts are linked by a Suzuki reaction, and the designed D-π-A (electron donating-π conjugation-electron withdrawing) structure is fabricated. Previously, naphthalene and pyrene were selected as the electron-donating groups. [7,8] The two designed structures qualified partly twisted intramolecular charge transfer and AIE characteristics. To deeply understand the influence of numbers of conjugated aromatic rings and rotation angles between the donating group and π unit, anthracene is chosen to the fabricate D-π-A structure. The designed NIFA structure is expected to exhibit the largest rotation angle. Thus, the satisfied AIE property could be observed based on the RIR mechanism. Because the alkyl chains are introduced and decorated on the fluorenyl unit, NIFA can easily dissolve in common organic solvents, such as dichloromethane, tetrahydrofuran, and acetonitrile, among others. The synthesized chemical structure of NIFA was thoroughly confirmed by 1H-NMR, 13C-NMR, HR-MS and HPLC (Supporting Information, Figs. S1–S4). The results proved that the synthesized NIFA is in agreement with the designed structure.

The photophysical properties of NIFA were studied in CH2Cl2 and are summarized in Table 1 . As shown in Fig. 1 , in the concentration range of 1 × 10−6 – 5 × 10−4 mol·L-1, the UV–vis absorption of NIFA in CH2Cl2 solution follows Beer’s law, suggesting no ground-state aggregation in this concentration range. NIFA shows a major absorption band (ε max > 30,000 L·mol-1 cm-1) between 325 and 425 nm. It can be assigned to the 1π-π* transition mixed with the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) transition. This assignment was confirmed by DFT and TD-DFT calculations. The DFT calculation is shown in Table 2 , the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of NIFA locates on the anthracene motif, and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) mostly locates on the 1,8-naphthalimide motif. Like most d-π-A molecules, such totally separated charge distribution leads to polarization of the chromophore and generation of a molecular dipole, which implies that ICT appears in the NIFA.

By checking the TD-DFT results (Fig. S5) and taking the oscillator strength (Table S1) into consideration, the mixed 1π-π*/ICT band could be assigned. The ICT is caused by the charge transfer from the fluorene part to the 1,8-naphthalimide part, corresponding to the HOMO-2→LUMO and HOMO-1→LUMO transitions. The 1π-π* transition is caused by the electron located within the 1,8-naphthalimide part (HOMO-2→LUMO and HOMO-1→LUMO) and anthracene part (HOMO→LUMO+1), separately. As shown in Fig. S6, the single bond connected 9-position anthracene and fluorene parts twist 84.3° and the two groups are nearly perpendicular, which means that it is scarcely possible for electrons to transfer from two parts and rarely transfer from anthracene to the 1,8-naphthalimide part. In contrast, the single bond connected 1,8-naphthalimide and fluorene parts twist 54.2°. Taking the strong electron-withdrawing ability of 1,8-naphthalimide into consideration, the electron in the fluorene part could easily transfer to the 1,8-naphthalimide part. All of these calculated results are consistent with the experiment results.

Next, the fluorescent emission spectra of NIFA in different solvents are shown in Fig. 2 . The corresponding photophysical parameters are listed in Table S2. The excitation wavelength is chosen at 365 nm and broad structure-less emission bands are monitored for the maximum emission peaks from 443 to 532 nm in different solvents. The NIFA solution color changes from blue to blue-green. When the solvent polarity is decreased, the emission bands dramatically blue shift and the quantum yield significantly increases. For example, when NIFA is dissolved in CH2Cl2, the maximum emission peak at 532 nm and the quantum yield is 0.082. When the solvent changes to hexane (non-polarity solvent), the maximum emission peak is blue-shifted to 443 nm and the quantum yield is increased to 0.598. The solvatochromic effect induced emission shift is as large as ∼3667 cm−1, which indicates that the emission bands arise from ICT. On the contrary, no significant solvatochromic effect was observed for the absorption of NIFA in non-polar to polar solvents in Fig. S7. Following, the emission lifetimes in different solvents were also measured, which could be fitted from single exponential fitting to biexponential fitting when decreasing the solvent polarity (Fig.S8 and Table S2). This could be ascribed to the increased vibronic energy exited state in low-polar solvents (hexane and toluene), which causes electrons to return to the ground state, resulting in different lifetimes. Then, a low temperature (77 K) luminescence technique is applied for NIFA in butyronitrile to explore the emission excited state. In Table 1 and Fig. S9, compared with an emission band at 293 K, a narrower structure-less band at 77 K with a thermal Stokes shift of 1334 cm−1 is monitored. This also indicates the ICT emission from the NIFA. Furthermore, compared with NIFA in the CH2Cl2, its thin film presents a narrower blue-shifted emission band at 500 nm in Fig. S10. However, the solid quantum yield of NIFA is up to 0.60, which is much brighter than in polar solvents. This indicates that NIFA could supply the AEE.

The AEE characteristic of NIFA is displayed in Fig. 3 . Firstly, NIFA was dissolved in CH3CN (CH3CN is mutually soluble with H2O). A weak emission was observed under an excitation wavelength at 365 nm. When H2O was added to the CH3CN solution, the emission intensity enhanced dramatically. After adding 5% volume water, the emission peak red shifted from 488 to 511 nm. This phenomenon is also in agreement with the solvent effect of the ICT donated organic compound. When the water fraction was enhanced to 40% volume, the emission intensity reached the highest point and the fluorescence color changed from blue to green blue. This could be ascribed to the RIR feature. Unfortunately, when we further added H2O, the emission intensity decreased (Fig. S11). Two possible reasons could explain the decreased fluorescent intensity under a certain water fraction solvent system. One is the formation of NIFA nanoparticles (NPs). Photons could not irradiate to the NP cores to excite more molecules. Another reason is the formation of π-π stacking in the aggregated NIFA NPs, which causes ACQ. To further study the aggregated NIFA NPs, dynamic light scattering (DLS) was used to measure the hydrate particle size. After analyzing the DLS data in Fig. S12, the average diameter of the NIFA NPs is ca. 145 nm (10% CH3CN, 90% H2O). This proves that a nanostructure in the aggregated state is formed.

Based on the AEE and hydrophobic properties of NIFA, a new detection technology is used for fingermark fluorescent imaging. Firstly, the volunteer’s sebaceous fingermark on the glass substrate is prepared. In Fig. S13, after incubating with NIFA solution, and exciting with a 365 nm UV lamp, the fluorescent fingermark fail to be seen clearly by the naked eye and captured by digital camera. Only a part of the ridge fluorescent fingermark imaging could be observed. More imaging details are missing and blocked by residual NIFA NPs. It is reported that the sebaceous on the fingermark surface could interact with superglue vapor to form a porous polymer in order to fix the fingermark. For improving the fingermark fluorescent imaging quality, a superglue fuming technique was used to pretreat sebaceous fingermarks. As shown in Fig. 4 , after pretreating with superglue vapor and incubating with NIFA solution, a clear fluorescent fingermark image not only can be observed by the naked eye, but also can be recorded by a common digital camera and an EMCCD camera. This technique supplies a convenient, fast, cheap and easy real-time visualization method for LFPs from crime scenes.

To investigate the difference between pretreatment and non-pretreatment with superglue, we used SEM to observe the fine structure of the fingermark. The corresponding SEM images are presented in Fig. 5 . Without the pretreatment of superglue fuming, the surface of the fingermark ridge area is easy to split away from the glass substrate after incubating with NIFA solution. This phenomenon can help explain why some details of the fingermark fluorescent image are missed in Fig. S13. In contrast, after pretreating with superglue fuming, the ridge of the fingermark could be fixed and maintained.

Based on the fact that pretreatment with superglue is more advantageous for improving the latent fingermark fluorescent imaging quality, we ascribe the reason for this to the strong hydrophobic-hydrophobic interaction between NIFA NPs and the cyanoacrylate polymer porous structure. To verify our explanation, hydrophobic NIFA NPs were co-assembled with phosphatidylcholine, F127 and amphipathy PEG to form core-shell NPs, as shown in Fig. S14. The mass ratios between the core and shell are 1:5 and 1:10. The DLS of these self-assembled NPs is presented in Fig. S15. After co-assembling, the surface of these self-assembled NPs becomes hydrophilic. We used these hydrophilic NPs to incubate with superglue pretreated fingermarks. Unfortunately, no clear fingermark fluorescent imaging could be distinguished in Fig. S16, even the outline of the fingermark could not be verified. The interaction between hydrophilic NPs and the hydrophobic polymer is too weak to image the latent fingermark. Therefore, we believe that the combined utilization of the cyanoacrylate fuming technique and hydrophobic luminescent NPs can enhance the latent fingermark fluorescent imaging quality.

Furthermore, fingermark imaging is not only carried out on glass substrates but is also carried out on frequently used daily necessities, such as aluminum foil, spoons, soft drink labels and so on. Thus, the extended application of the NIFA solution for the fluorescence imaging of the fumed LFPs on various substrates was carried out, as shown in Fig. 6 . Under sunlight, LFPs on different substrates are invisible. After fuming and incubating with NIFA solution, bright fluorescence can be observed under UV light. The images exhibit high contrast between the fluorescent ridges and none fluorescent furrows, which improve the imaging quality and make fingermark more distinguishable. In the field of forensic chemistry and crime detection, three levels of features are utilized to recognize fingermark details. [47] For the first level, macroscopic features could be monitored, such as whorls, double loops, arches and so on. These fingermarks are identifiable, but the information is insufficient. The examination method for comparative identification contains analysis, comparison, evaluation and verification procedure, which is well established and used. [47] To determine the sensitivity of NIFA probes, the enlarged view of these fingermark fluorescence images reveals second level specific ridge details. We amplify the images and the results are shown in Fig. 6(1–12). Subsequent level details at different fingermark regions, such as hooks, bifurcations, loops, short ridges and so on, are easily visualized.

Commonly, first and second level fingermark structural information is enough for forensic identification. However, the fine structure (high resolution) of the third level of LFPs provides quantitative data for accurate fingermarks recognition, including details of line shape, and sweat pores, among others. It is not easy to capture the high-resolution third level fingermark image with any other reported methods. [40,42,44] Herein, we used a stereomicroscope combined with an EMCCD camera and portable UV lamp to acquire high resolution latent fingermark third level images. As illustrated in Fig. 7 , the fine structures of sweat pores, line shapes and ridge trends, all third level details, are presented. In Fig. S17, the quantification of the luminescence intensity in Fig. 7 was measured, the fluorescent intensity ratio between fingermark ridge and fingermark furrow is ca. 5.7-7.7. Overall, the high contrast and high resolution fingermark fluorescent image is clearly observed by the combined method of superglue fuming and fluorescent imaging, which means NIFA for the latent fingermark detection and imaging is practicable, and the combined method can provide more valuable fingermark information for personal identification.

In summary, we synthesized a 1,8-naphthalimide based luminescent material NIFA with an AEE. The photophysical characteristics of NIFA are well explored by an experimental and theoretical study. In the absorption study, the major absorption band is ascribed to the 1π-π*/ICT mixed states. Under excitation at 365 nm, NIFA can emit tunable color from blue to green-blue with high quantum yields. Further application study proves that NIFA as an effective probe can be used for the latent fingermark fluorescent detection and imaging. Combined with a cyanoacrylate glue fuming technique and fluorescent imaging method, excellent fingermark images were acquired. The mechanism study reveals that strong hydrophobic-hydrophobic interaction between superglue polymer and NIFA NPs can enhance fingermark fluorescent image quality. Further study reveals that this combined method not only can image LFPs on different substrates (including glass, aluminum foil, spoon, and soft drink label), but also can acquire second-/third-level high contrast and high resolution images of fingermarks. This work supplies a convenient, cheap and easy operation method for latent fingermarks acquisition in the criminal investigation and forensic evidence identification fields. We expect that it could be applied in other fluorescent imaging related fields in the foreseeable future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21501121, 21501029), Science and Technology Development Projects of USST (2017KJFZ114, 2018KJFZ147), Natural Science Foundation of Shanghai (15ZR1438600) and Shanghai Rising-Star Program (17QA1403500).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.12.002.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Revision A

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yuhao Li received his Ph.D. in applied chemistry from Nanjing University of Technology, China in 2011. He is currently working as an associate professor at University of Shanghai for Science and Technology. His research interests are nonlinear optical materials, nanomaterials-based sensors, and biomaterials.

Yun Sun received his Ph.D. in inorganic chemistry from Fudan University, China in 2012. He is currently working as a principle investigator at Shanghai Proton and Heavy Ion Center. His research interests are inorganic nanomaterials, biomaterials.

Yong Deng received his science bachelor in applied chemistry in 2017 from University of Shanghai for Science and Technology. He is working for his master’s degree in University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, China. His research interests are self-assembled organic materials and biomaterials.

Jie Liu received his engineering bachelor in chemistry engineering in 2017 from Changchun University of Technology. He is working for his master’s degree in University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, China. His research interests are inorganic nanomaterials and biomaterials.

Jingxin Fu is currently an undergraduate student in the major of applied chemistry from University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, China. His research interests are self-assembled organic materials and biomaterials.

Ruizhuo Ouyang received her Ph.D. in analytical chemistry from Nanjing University, China in 2008. She is currently working as an associate professor at University of Shanghai for Science and Technology. Her research interests are electrochemistry, immunoassay, and biomaterials.

Yuqing Miao is currently a professor at University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, China. He has abroad research interest, such as nano-catalysis materials, electrochemical analysis, biosensors, and biomaterials.
